preFix.title: The bridle joint
preFix.subtitle: How to mark out, saw, and fit a bridle joint for frame corners
preFix.excerpt: The bridle joint is a three-part joint used at the corners and T-junctions of frames: a central tenon fits into a matching open slot. It is stronger than a plain butt joint and faster to cut than a mortise-and-tenon. This technique tutorial covers the full marking-out and sawing sequence.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the bridle joint method and marking sequence; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the saw-and-chisel procedure.

content.text: is used wherever two frame members meet at a corner or T-junction: chair rails, picture frames, and light structural frames. The joint has three parts: the open slot (the fork) on one piece, and the central
on the other. Both are one-third the thickness of the stock. On 25 mm stock, the tenon is 8 mm thick; the slot cheeks are 8 mm each and the slot width is 8 mm.

on each piece: the best face and the best edge. All gauging is taken from these two faces. Do not mix gauging from different faces on the same joint. Set the marking gauge to one-third the stock thickness and score the tenon cheek lines on the tenon piece, working from the datum face and datum edge.

across the tenon piece with the combination square and the marking knife. The knife leaves a cleaner, sharper line than a pencil and the tenon saw will register in the knife cut. Mark the shoulder line on all four faces of the piece, working from the datum face and checking all four lines align.

Saw the tenon cheeks with the tenon saw, keeping the saw on the waste side of the gauge line. Saw down to the shoulder line only; stop at the knife line. On the fork piece, saw the two outer cheeks in the same way, then saw across the base of the slot from the face edge to remove the waste. The slot base cut is a crosscut; keep the saw vertical and check from the side as well as from the front.

Test-fit the joint by pushing the tenon into the slot by hand. The fit should be snug with no slop but not require a mallet. If the tenon is too tight, pare the cheeks with the chisel, taking thin shavings and testing after each pass. If too loose, the joint must be re-made; there is no practical way to tighten a too-loose bridle joint.
Gauging from inconsistent faces: always work from the same datum face for both pieces. Mixing faces produces a joint that is offset and will not close flush.
Sawing past the shoulder line: the tenon saw should stop exactly at the knife mark. Sawing past it leaves a short-grain stub at the shoulder that will break off.
Paring across the grain to clean the slot bottom: pare along the grain when cleaning the slot base. Across-grain paring tears the fibres rather than slicing them.

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
